_meta.framework: UNESCO AI Competency Framework for Teachers (AI CFT), 2024
_meta.study_scope: Pre-service teachers learning about decision trees as an AI/ML system through ProDaBi (unplugged) and CODAP Arbor (plugged) activities.
_meta.note_on_scope: This mapping covers ONLY the 'AI foundations and applications' competency (Aspect 3). The full AI CFT has 5 aspects. Pre-service teacher performance on AI pedagogy (Aspect 4) or professional development (Aspect 5) is NOT assessed through these worksheets.

progression_levels.Acquire: Competency 3.1 Basic AI techniques and applications — conceptual knowledge of how AI works, basic vocabulary, ability to operate validated AI tools.
progression_levels.Deepen: Competency 3.2 Application skills — proficiently operate AI tools, visually represent AI system functioning, transferable knowledge on data/algorithms.
progression_levels.Create: Competency 3.3 Creating with AI — customize or modify AI tools, evaluate AI system design, contribute to defining testing criteria.

Acquire.label: Acquire
Acquire.teacher_competency_description: Teachers acquire basic conceptual knowledge on AI, including the definition of AI, basic knowledge of how AI models are trained, associated knowledge on data and algorithms, main categories of AI technologies and examples of each, and the basic capacity to examine the appropriateness of specific AI tools for education and to operate validated AI tools.

relevant_learning_objectives.LO3.1.1: Demonstrate conceptual knowledge appropriate to their competencies and responsibilities on how AI systems are developed using data, algorithms and computing architecture; acquire relevant understanding and skills on data, algorithms and programming; and exemplify key steps including problem-scoping, design, training, testing, deployment, feedback and iteration.
relevant_learning_objectives.LO3.1.2: Exemplify what AI is and is not, the main categories of AI techniques and AI technologies, the novel capabilities that AI could actualize compared to previous generations of ICT tools, and the core functions of various categories of AI tools.
relevant_learning_objectives.LO3.1.3: Locate and operate AI tools that are necessary for their daily work in local contexts.
relevant_learning_objectives.LO3.1.4: Explain the importance of evaluating AI tools to ensure their accessibility, inclusivity, and reliability; undertake basic analyses of the appropriateness of specific AI tools for education in local contexts with particular attention to the impact on students with special needs.
relevant_learning_objectives.LO3.1.5: Start consolidating a personal collection of trustworthy AI tools that are necessary for life and work and relevant to the local language and culture.

Deepen.label: Deepen
Deepen.teacher_competency_description: Teachers are expected to proficiently operate AI tools adopted in educational settings; to deepen their knowledge of various categories of AI technologies and their practical skills concerning data and algorithms that are appropriate to teaching responsibilities and background competencies while infusing relevant ethical principles in practice.

relevant_learning_objectives.LO3.2.1: Proficiently operate commonly used AI tools in daily life and in education; exemplify the typical techniques used by these tools and explain their implications for education.
relevant_learning_objectives.LO3.2.2: Visually represent how selected AI systems work, including how they are trained and tested, as well as the typical models, algorithms, and datasets used.
relevant_learning_objectives.LO3.2.3: Demonstrate transferable knowledge on data, algorithms and coding and apply it to solve problems that are appropriate to their abilities and the remit of their role.

Create.label: Create
Create.teacher_competency_description: Teachers are able to customize or modify AI tools in a proficient manner, applying enhanced conceptual knowledge and operational skills to create AI-assisted inclusive learning environments, and address broader challenges in educational contexts.

relevant_learning_objectives.LO3.3.1: Demonstrate knowledge and skills on AI system design at the level of expert teachers as well as comprehensive competencies to analyse the limitations of selected AI systems in solving real-world problems in local educational contexts.
relevant_learning_objectives.LO3.3.2: Apply appropriate knowledge and skills on data, algorithms, programming and AI models to customize and/or assemble existing AI tools or semi-finished AI models to create AI tools or fine-tune open-source AI systems to create solutions that are both relevant and affordable for local settings and specific use cases.
relevant_learning_objectives.LO3.3.3: Revise or define criteria for the comprehensive testing of a self-created AI tool and for the purpose of optimization and further iteration of the tool.
relevant_learning_objectives.LO3.3.4: Contribute to a new or existing repository of user-created or tailored AI tools based on personal and institutional needs.

WS1_B1_to_B3.rationale: Core vocabulary for supervised learning — objects, features, labels. Knowing these is foundational to understanding how any ML model is trained.

WS1_B4_to_B5.rationale: Threshold and rule are the operational mechanisms of a decision tree. Knowing them demonstrates understanding of how DT makes decisions.

WS1_B6_to_B7.rationale: TP and FP are the building blocks of model evaluation. Correctly defining them shows the student understands how AI performance is measured.

WS1_B8_to_B9.rationale: Knowing evaluation formulas (sensitivity, MCR) maps to LO3.1.1: understanding how AI systems are evaluated and iterated.

WS1_B10.rationale: Overfitting is a more advanced concept. Correctly defining it (training vs test generalization) signals readiness for Deepen. If only memorized without understanding, stays at Acquire.

WS1_B11.rationale: Defining a decision tree as a data-learning model maps to LO3.1.2 — exemplifying what AI is and identifying AI categories.

WS3_B1_to_B6.rationale: Applying a given threshold rule to classify items = operating a validated AI tool at the most basic level (LO3.1.3). Understanding the if-then logic is LO3.1.1.

WS3_B7_to_B8.rationale: Creating own threshold (with ≤/> notation) for a specified variable shows student can parameterize a simple AI rule.

WS4_B1.rationale: Drawing the improved threshold between avocado and french fries applies threshold optimisation on sorted food cards.

WS4_B2.rationale: Marking all four misclassified foods at the prior threshold shows error-pattern recognition.

WS4_B3.rationale: Stating that misclassification decreased links threshold change to model performance.

WS4_B4.rationale: Agreeing Pia is haklı because apple and raspberry jam share the same fat value explains why no split is possible between them.

WS4_B5.rationale: Stating a learned energy threshold (160–2223) from the cards applies optimisation to a second feature.

WS5_grid.rationale: Systematically trying multiple threshold values for a chosen feature and computing MCR is a core Deepen activity. It requires proficient operation of the tool (LO3.2.1) and demonstrates understanding of the training mechanism (LO3.2.2).

WS5_B25.rationale: Selecting and justifying the best threshold based on MCR comparison is the key Deepen behavior: making data-driven model decisions.

WS6_tree_structure.rationale: Drawing and documenting a two-level decision tree structure maps directly to LO3.2.2: visually representing how an AI system works, including its training structure.

concepts: reading tree paths as if-then rules
WS7_part1_path_matching.rationale: Matching given rules to tree paths tests whether students can read a decision tree as a set of conditional statements — core Acquire literacy.

WS7_part2_own_rules.rationale: Formulating rules from their own tree crosses into Deepen if the student can express the logic of their own model clearly. Mere recall of the tree structure stays at Acquire.

WS10_table.rationale: Computing midpoints between adjacent values and evaluating each is a hands-on implementation of the threshold search algorithm. This is LO3.2.3 — demonstrating transferable knowledge on algorithms.

WS11_B8a.rationale: Using a provided tree to classify a new item = operating a validated AI tool (LO3.1.3).

WS11_B8b.rationale: Articulating the decision rule used = being able to represent the AI system's logic.

WS11_Q10.rationale: Correctly identifying what a DT can and cannot do maps to LO3.1.2 — exemplifying what AI is and is not, what it can do compared to other tools.

WS11_Q11.rationale: Ordering the steps (feature selection → data arrangement → threshold → decision) demonstrates understanding of the training process (LO3.1.1: key steps).

WS11_Q12.rationale: Understanding why DT qualifies as AI (automatic decision-making, computer-buildable) vs common misconceptions (computer thinks, never wrong) maps to LO3.1.2.

DT_A_Q1.rationale: Prior belief question — no wrong answer, assesses initial awareness.

DT_A_Q2.rationale: Data-driven variable analysis = proficient operation of the tool to form hypotheses.

DT_A_Q4.rationale: Justifying variable choice with data evidence signals Deepen reasoning.

DT_B_Q4.rationale: Comparing three single-feature trees using metrics = proficient operation + data interpretation.

DT_C_Q2.rationale: Explaining how threshold changes affect performance shows understanding of the DT algorithm's mechanism.

DT_D_Q2.rationale: Metric-based comparison of depth-1 vs depth-2 tree.

DT_D_Q4.rationale: Principled stopping criterion shows understanding of model optimization — early Create signal if student references overfitting.

DT_E_sensitivity_MCR_fillin.rationale: Filling in metric values = applying known formulas to own data.

DT_E_Q1.rationale: Metric interpretation with reference to purpose.

DT_E_Q4.rationale: Recognizing that perfect classification is impossible due to data overlap is a core AI literacy insight — it maps to LO3.3.1 (analyzing limitations of AI systems).

DT_F_Q2.rationale: Understanding train/test performance difference as overfitting maps to LO3.3.1 — analyzing limitations. This is the strongest Create signal in the unplugged/plugged sequence.

DT_G_Q1.rationale: Reflecting on what the model 'learned' requires understanding AI as a learning system, not just a tool — this is a Create-level insight (LO3.3.1).

DT_G_Q2.rationale: Rich metacognitive reflection naming specific concepts (threshold, overfitting, train/test) signals Create-level understanding. Thin answers ('I learned the program') stay at Acquire.

minimum_evidence: WS1: at least 7 of 11 concept definitions correct (especially nesne, özellik, etiket, eşik değeri, kural)
WS11: Q10 mostly correct (identifies what DT can/cannot do)
WS11: Q12 correct (why DT is AI)

Deepen.prerequisite: Acquire level evidence must be present
minimum_evidence: WS4: Pia evaluation correct with reasoning (B4)
WS5: At least 3 threshold trials with arithmetic consistency AND data-based B25
WS_DT sections B and C: metric-based variable and threshold selection
WS_DT D Q2: quantitative comparison of tree depths
WS_DT E Q1: metric interpretation with purpose reference

Create.prerequisite: Deepen level evidence must be present
minimum_evidence: WS_DT E Q4: correctly states DT cannot achieve perfect classification with reasoning
WS_DT F Q2: explains overfitting / train-test gap
WS_DT G Q1 or G Q2: rich reflection naming specific AI concepts

level_assignment_rules.level_hierarchy: Levels are cumulative. A student at Create must also show Deepen and Acquire evidence. A student showing only some Deepen signals but missing Acquire foundations is assigned Acquire.
level_assignment_rules.partial_level: If evidence is mixed (some Deepen signals but foundational Acquire items wrong), assign the lower level and flag as 'approaching_[higher_level]'.
